A $5,000 quote and a $15,000 quote for the same project aren’t just different numbers — they’re usually scoping completely different things. Here’s how to tell the difference.

A founder recently sent me two proposals for their Shopify store redesign. One came in at $4,500. The other at $14,000. Same brief. Same deliverables on the surface.
They asked me which one was right.
The honest answer: neither of them had scoped the project correctly — and that’s why the numbers were so far apart. The $4,500 quote was almost certainly leaving half the work out. The $14,000 quote was almost certainly including work the client didn’t need yet.
Bad scoping is the single biggest source of conflict in web design projects. It’s why projects run over budget. Why clients feel overcharged. Why designers feel underpaid. Why timelines slip. Why relationships sour at the end of an otherwise good engagement.
Here’s how I scope projects — and why the process matters more than the number at the bottom of the quote.

The Root Problem: Quoting the Output, Not the Outcome
Most web design quotes are built around deliverables:

• Homepage design

• 5 interior pages

• Mobile responsive

• 2 rounds of revisions
This looks like a complete scope. It isn’t. It describes what will be produced, not what needs to happen for the project to succeed.
The problem with output-based scoping is that it creates a false equivalence between projects that are fundamentally different. Two clients can both ask for “a homepage design” and mean completely different things:
One client has a brand guide, approved copy, high-resolution photography, and a clear conversion goal. The other needs help developing the brand direction, writing the copy, sourcing or creating imagery, and defining what the page should actually accomplish.
Same deliverable. Completely different project. Output-based quotes either charge them the same (which means losing money on the second client) or quote them differently without being able to explain why.

How I Actually Scope a Project
Before I put a number on anything, I need answers to five questions. These aren’t just intake form questions — they determine the actual shape and cost of the work.

Question 1: What does success look like in 90 days?
This is the most important question and the one most briefs don’t answer.
“A new website” is not a success metric. “A website that converts 2% of visitors to booking inquiries” is. “A Shopify store that does $30k in the first month” is. “A site our sales team can use in client meetings without being embarrassed” is.
The answer to this question tells me whether the project is primarily a design problem, a conversion problem, a content problem, or a credibility problem — and each of those requires different work.
If a client can’t answer this question, that’s valuable information too. It usually means they need a discovery phase before any design work begins.

Question 2: What assets exist, and what needs to be created?
This question doubles or halves project scope more often than any other.
Assets I’m checking for:

• Brand guidelines (logo, colours, typography — documented or undocumented)

• Copy (who is writing it, who is approving it, what’s the review process)

• Photography and imagery (existing library, new shoot required, stock)

• Content structure (do they know what pages they need, or does that need to be defined)
A project where everything exists and just needs to be designed is a very different project from one where I’m working with a logo on a business card and a verbal description of the brand. Both are real projects. Neither should be quoted the same way.

Question 3: How many decision-makers are involved?
This one sounds administrative. It isn’t.
A project with one decision-maker who can approve work quickly moves in a straight line. A project with three stakeholders who need to align before anything is approved moves in spirals — and every spiral costs time.
Multiple decision-makers means:

• Longer feedback cycles

• More rounds of revision to satisfy different opinions

• A higher likelihood of scope creep as different stakeholders introduce new requirements late in the process

• More project management overhead throughout
I price for this. A project with a committee of approvers carries a time overhead that a single-decision-maker project simply doesn’t — and the quote needs to reflect it.

Question 4: What platform and what integrations?
Not all Shopify builds are equal. A standard theme customisation is not the same project as a headless Next.js build with Sanity CMS and Shopify Storefront API integration, even if both result in “a Shopify store.”
I’m also checking integrations — not because they’re always complex, but because they’re often assumed rather than specified:

• Email marketing (Klaviyo setup, list migration, flow configuration)

• Reviews (Judge.me or Loox installation and import of existing reviews)

• Analytics (GA4, Meta Pixel, conversion tracking setup)

• Payment methods (Afterpay, Klarna, Shop Pay configuration)

• Inventory or ERP connections
Each of these can be a half-day of work or a three-day integration depending on the client’s existing setup. A quote that ignores them is a quote that will run over.

Question 5: What happens after launch?
The launch is not the end of the project. For most clients, it’s the beginning of the part they haven’t thought about yet.

• Who updates the site after it goes live?

• Is there a content management workflow the client can handle independently, or do they need a developer for every change?

• Is there a maintenance or retainer arrangement, or is this a one-time handover?
The answer shapes what I build. A site that needs to be maintained by a non-technical founder needs a very different CMS setup than one that will be managed by an in-house developer. Building the wrong thing creates support burden on my side and frustration on theirs.

The Scoping Document
Once I have answers to all five questions, I build a scoping document before the quote. Not a contract — just a plain-English summary of what the project actually is:

• What we’re building and why

• What assets exist and what needs to be created

• What’s included and what’s explicitly out of scope

• What I need from the client and when

• The milestones and payment structure
This document does more to prevent project conflict than any contract clause. When both sides agree on what the project is before it starts, there’s almost nothing to argue about later.
It also surfaces misalignment early — before money changes hands and before expectations have hardened. If a client reads the scoping document and says “wait, I thought copy was included” — that’s a conversation I want to have on day one, not week six.

How the Quote Is Structured
Once the scope is defined, the quote follows naturally from the actual work required — not from what feels like a competitive market rate.
I structure every quote in three milestones:
Milestone 1 — Discovery and Design (40%) Paid upfront before work begins. Covers research, wireframes, and design direction. This milestone ends with an approved design the client has seen and signed off on. No code has been written, which means changes at this stage are low-cost.
Milestone 2 — Development and Integration (40%) Paid when development begins. Covers the build, integrations, content population, and internal QA. This milestone ends with a staging environment the client can review fully.
Milestone 3 — Launch and Handover (20%) Paid on or before launch. Covers final revisions, launch support, and handover documentation. The reduced final payment reflects the fact that the heavy work is done — and gives the client confidence they’re not paying in full for something they haven’t yet seen working.
This structure protects both sides. I’m not carrying the full project risk. The client isn’t paying in full for something they haven’t seen. Both of us have a financial stake in moving through milestones cleanly.

What This Means for the Client Getting Two Quotes
Back to the founder with the $4,500 and $14,000 proposals.
The right question isn’t which number is fair. It’s: does each quote account for the same scope?
Ask both designers:

• Have you factored in copywriting, or are you assuming I provide it?

• Does this include email and analytics integrations, or is that separate?

• How many rounds of revisions are included, and what counts as a revision?

• What happens if the project runs over the estimated timeline?
If the $4,500 quote can answer all of those cleanly and account for the full scope — it might be genuinely efficient. More likely, it’s missing something that will show up as a change request halfway through.
The cheapest quote is rarely the cheapest project. The most useful number is the one built on the most honest scope.

The Broader Point
Pricing is a communication problem before it’s a numbers problem. When a quote is wrong — too low, too high, or too far from another quote to be compared — it’s almost always because the scope wasn’t defined clearly enough for the number to mean anything.
Define the scope first. Build the quote from the scope. Show your working.
That process won’t always win the project. But it will consistently attract clients who understand what they’re buying — and those are the only projects worth taking.

Vahid is a freelance web designer and developer specializing in Shopify and Next.js storefronts for luxury brands and e-commerce businesses globally.
